---
title: "Lawley 2022 continental Zn-Pb benchmark — reproduced and audited"
subtitle: "Reproducing AUC 0.983 on 5.16M H3 hexagons, then accounting for where the published number overstates real generalization"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
---

## The experiment

Lawley et al. (2022, *Ore Geology Reviews*) trained a gradient-boosted
decision-tree model to predict sediment-hosted lead-zinc mineralization
across the United States, Canada, and Australia. They divided the
three continents into 5.16 million hexagonal cells (about 5 km² each)
and assembled ninety-seven features per cell: gravity and magnetic
geophysical responses at multiple spatial scales, seismic
depth-to-Moho and depth-to-LAB, lithology and geological-period
dictionaries, paleo-latitude, and proximity-to-feature columns for
terrane boundaries, passive margins, black shales, and faults. The
published model reported an AUC of 0.983 — meaning a randomly chosen
real-deposit cell scored higher than a randomly chosen non-deposit
cell 98.3 percent of the time.

SRI International published a public Jupyter reimplementation of the
paper as part of the DARPA-CRITICALMAAS program. We reproduced the
experiment using the public code on the public data, then layered
three orthogonal tightenings on the published evaluation (a label-leak
fix, a stricter spatial cross-validation, and a held-out cross-
continent transfer test), and ran the DEEP-SEAM deep-anomaly-detection
architecture on the same benchmark for a like-for-like comparison.
The work establishes a quantitative breakdown of where the published
0.983 number comes from and where it overstates generalization.

## How the algorithm consumes the data

The gradient-boosted-trees algorithm consumes the ninety-seven-column
feature stack by iteratively building shallow decision trees. Each
tree node asks a threshold question of one feature ("is distance to
the nearest passive margin less than 50 kilometers" or "is the Bouguer
gravity at this cell above the regional median"), and a new tree is
added at each iteration to correct the residual errors of the
previous trees. The final score for a cell is a weighted sum across
all trees. The architecture is good at learning conditional
multivariate rules of the form "prospective if sedimentary host rock
AND near black shale AND near fault," which is exactly the geological
recipe the Lawley feature stack encodes.

## Results: the cumulative accounting

```{python}
#| label: results-table
#| code-fold: true
import json
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

base = Path("../../data/derived/lawley")
p1 = json.loads((base / "path1_baseline_metrics.json").read_text())
p1b = json.loads((base / "path1b_leak_corrected_metrics.json").read_text())
p2 = json.loads((base / "path2_tightened_eval_metrics.json").read_text())

rows = [
    ("Lawley 2022 published", "—", 0.983, "—", "—"),
    ("Phase 1: reproduction (leak in)",
     "Lawley 1-D latitude-band CV", p1["auc_test"],
     f"{p1['n_train']:,}", f"{p1['n_test']:,}"),
    ("Phase 1b: leak corrected",
     "Lawley 1-D latitude-band CV", p1b["auc_test"],
     f"{p1b['n_train']:,}", f"{p1b['n_test']:,}"),
    ("Phase 2: 2-D blocked CV",
     "12 x 12 lat x lon quantile blocks", p2["cv_2d_blocks"]["auc"]["mean"],
     "~4.1 M", "~1.0 M"),
    ("Phase 2: USCAN -> Australia transfer",
     "Train US/Canada, predict Australia", p2["transfer_uscan_to_aus"]["auc"]["mean"],
     "3,620,022", "1,544,811"),
    ("Phase 2: Australia -> USCAN transfer",
     "Train Australia, predict US/Canada", p2["transfer_aus_to_uscan"]["auc"]["mean"],
     "1,544,811", "3,620,022"),
]
df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Phase", "CV scheme", "Test AUC", "Train cells", "Test cells"])
df["Test AUC"] = df["Test AUC"].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.4f}" if isinstance(x, float) else x)
df
```

The four numbers together (0.997 → 0.972 → 0.868 → 0.71 → 0.56)
account for the entire gap between the published score and an honest
measurement of cross-region generalization. The 0.87 number under 2-D
spatial blocks is real geological signal, mostly carried by the
proximity-to-black-shale, proximity-to-passive-margin, and
sedimentary-host-rock dictionary features. The leak-and-evaluation
findings do not invalidate the paper's geological insight; they put
the right error bars on its headline number.

## Interesting finding 1: performance engineering required to make the experiment run on consumer hardware

The published Lawley CSV is **7.2 GB**. Loaded naively with pandas,
the in-memory representation expands to roughly **25 GB** because
pandas defaults to object dtypes for string-typed columns. On a 16 GB
machine this triggers hours of swap-bound thrashing.

Three optimizations, applied in order:

### Optimization 1: CSV streamed → parquet

The fix was to stream the CSV through a chunked pyarrow `ParquetWriter`,
casting "Present" / "Absent" string columns to boolean and 64-bit
floats to 32-bit floats during the stream. The output is a 1.2 GB
compressed parquet that loads in five seconds. The conversion itself
runs in 2.3 minutes on the same hardware, with peak RAM during
conversion of ~600 MB.

| Stage | Before | After | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disk size | 7.2 GB CSV | 1.2 GB parquet | 6× smaller |
| Load time | hours (swap-bound) | 5 seconds | >720× |
| RAM during load | exceeds 16 GB | ~3 GB | fits |

### Optimization 2: vectorized `neighbor_deposits`

The published `neighbor_deposits` feature helper used a row-wise
pandas apply combined with a quadratic-time list-deduplication step.
On the 3.6-million-row US + Canada subset, it had run for 42 minutes
on the first attempt without completing before being killed.

The fix replaced each of the four phases with vectorized pandas or
numpy primitives: numpy boolean OR over the underlying bool arrays
for step 1; `.str[10:-2].str.split(", ")` for step 2;
Python `set.update()` for step 3 (eliminating the O(N²) list scan);
`.explode().isin().groupby(level=0).any()` for step 4.

| Region | SRI original runtime | Vectorized runtime |
|---|---|---|
| Australia (1.5M rows) | did not complete | 6 seconds |
| US + Canada (3.6M rows) | did not complete in 42 minutes | 19 seconds |

Lower bound on speedup: ≥130× on the US + Canada subset alone.

### Optimization 3: `pd.factorize` replaces dict-replace categorical encoding

The published `convert_categorical` helper used
`pd.Series.replace(dict)` to map string categories to integer codes.
That operation iterates row-wise in Python; on a 5-million-row
column it takes about 5 minutes per column, and the loop runs over
4-5 columns. `pd.factorize` is the C-level implementation of the
same operation. Replacing the call dropped per-column encoding from
~5 minutes to under one second — roughly **100× speedup**.

End-to-end, the experiment now runs in **about 3 minutes** on the
same 16 GB laptop. The takeaway is the gap between published
research code and operationally usable code is often a fixed amount
of unglamorous engineering, and the engineering is what makes the
science reproducible.

## Interesting finding 2: where the published 0.983 actually comes from

The reproduction matches the published number to within 0.014. The
gap between the reproduction (0.9965) and the "honest" generalization
number breaks down as follows:

### The label leak (2.4 pp)

The published `preferred` feature configuration includes the column
`Training_MVT_Occurrence` as an input feature, and the target the
model is trained on is computed as the logical OR of
`Training_MVT_Deposit` and `Training_MVT_Occurrence`. One of the
inputs arithmetically defines half of the target. Dropping that
column from the feature set produces an AUC of 0.972 instead of
0.997 on the same held-out fold.

### 1-D vs 2-D spatial blocking (9 pp)

The published evaluation isolates the held-out fold by latitude
only, so cells in the test strip are typically a few hundred
kilometers from training cells with similar geology immediately east
or west. The 2-D quantile-grid evaluation isolates the test fold
across both axes. AUC drops from 0.958 to 0.868 with bootstrap 95%
CI [0.848, 0.887].

### Cross-continent transfer (16 pp)

Training on the US + Canada cube and asking the model to score
Australia produces AUC 0.71 with CI [0.677, 0.741]. The reverse
direction (train Australia, score US + Canada) collapses to AUC
0.557 with CI [0.544, 0.570] — where 0.5 would be random guessing.
The "top 1 percent" of cells the model ranks most prospective
contains only 2 percent of the real deposits in the harder direction;
the published model has not learned anything generalizable enough
to surface deposits in a continent it has not seen.

## What the model is actually learning (when the leak is removed)

The 0.87 AUC under 2-D spatial blocks is real geological signal. The
features doing the most work are the proximity-to-feature columns
(black shale, passive margin, fault) and the sedimentary-host-rock
dictionary aggregate. These features encode the published MVT
mineral-system model directly: sediment-hosted lead-zinc deposits
form in carbonate basins near reduced source rocks, on passive
continental margins, along structurally-prepared fluid pathways.
The model is learning the recipe, and the recipe carries genuine
signal at the continental scale.

What the model is *not* learning is something general enough to
predict deposits in a continent it has not seen. The Australia →
USCAN transfer collapse demonstrates that the model has learned
local pattern-matching, not transferable geological theory. That
distinction is important for exploration use: a model that produces
top-1% capture inside its training continent but coin-flip
predictions outside it is useful for areas you have already drilled
and useful in a narrower way than the published number suggests for
areas you have not.

## Files and reproduction

- Phase 1 driver: [scripts/lawley_phase1_gbm.py](../../scripts/lawley_phase1_gbm.py)
- Phase 1b driver: [scripts/lawley_phase1b_leak_corrected.py](../../scripts/lawley_phase1b_leak_corrected.py)
- Phase 2 driver: [scripts/lawley_phase2_eval.py](../../scripts/lawley_phase2_eval.py)
- CSV → parquet converter: [scripts/lawley_csv_to_parquet.py](../../scripts/lawley_csv_to_parquet.py)
- Reference code (SRI baseline): [`DARPA-CRITICALMAAS/sri-ta3-baselines`](https://github.com/DARPA-CRITICALMAAS/sri-ta3-baselines)
- Findings writeups: [Phase 1](../../research/lawley_phase1_findings_2026-05.md), [Phase 2](../../research/lawley_phase2_findings_2026-05.md)

## Sources

- Lawley, C. J. M. et al. 2022. Data-driven prospectivity modelling of sediment-hosted Zn-Pb mineral systems and their critical raw materials. *Ore Geology Reviews* 141, 104635.
- USGS data release [`619552fbd34eb622f6906b26`](https://data.usgs.gov/datacatalog/data/USGS:619552fbd34eb622f6906b26). Prospectivity models for the United States, Canada, and Australia.
- NRCan GeoScan publication R=329203, DOI 10.4095/329203. The underlying H3 data cube.
- DARPA-CRITICALMAAS program, USGS/DARPA AI for Critical Minerals (AI4M).
